name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ كَلِمَةٍ سَوَآءٍۭ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ وَلَا نُشْرِكَ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنَا بَعْضًا أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُولُوا۟ ٱشْهَدُوا۟ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ (٦٤)
```

```english
Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that some of us will not take some others as lords besides Allah.’ But if they turn away, say, ‘Be witnesses that we have submitted [to Allah].’ (64)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تُحَآجُّونَ فِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَتِ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ وَٱلْإِنجِيلُ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٥)
```

```english
O People of the Book! Why do you argue concerning Abraham? Neither the Torah nor the Evangel were sent down until [long] after him. Do you not exercise your reason? (65)
```

```arabic
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ حَـٰجَجْتُمْ فِيمَا لَكُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ فَلِمَ تُحَآجُّونَ فِيمَا لَيْسَ لَكُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٦)
```

```english
Ah! You are the very ones who argue about that of which you have knowledge. But why do you argue about that of which you have no knowledge? And Allah knows and you do not know. (66)
```

```arabic
مَا كَانَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ يَهُودِيًّا وَلَا نَصْرَانِيًّا وَلَـٰكِن كَانَ حَنِيفًا مُّسْلِمًا وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٦٧)
```

```english
Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian. Rather, he was a Hanif, a Muslim, and he was not one of the polytheists. (67)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ أَوْلَى ٱلنَّاسِ بِإِبْرَٰهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُ وَهَـٰذَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٦٨)
```

```english
Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who follow him and this prophet and those who have faith, and Allah is the guardian of the faithful. (68)
```

```arabic
وَدَّت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَوْ يُضِلُّونَكُمْ وَمَا يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٦٩)
```

```english
A group of the People of the Book were eager to lead you astray; yet they lead no one astray except themselves, but they are not aware. (69)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَنتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ (٧٠)
```

```english
O People of the Book! Why do you deny Allah’s signs while you testify [to their truth]? (70)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَلْبِسُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَتَكْتُمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٧١)
```

```english
O People of the Book! Why do you mix the truth with falsehood, and conceal the truth while you know [it]? (71)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَجْهَ ٱلنَّهَارِ وَٱكْفُرُوٓا۟ ءَاخِرَهُۥ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٧٢)
```

```english
A group of the People of the Book say, ‘Believe in what has been sent down to the faithful at the beginning of the day, and disbelieve at its end, so that they may turn back [from their religion].’ (72)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِمَن تَبِعَ دِينَكُمْ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْهُدَىٰ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ أَن يُؤْتَىٰٓ أَحَدٌ مِّثْلَ مَآ أُوتِيتُمْ أَوْ يُحَآجُّوكُمْ عِندَ رَبِّكُمْ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْفَضْلَ بِيَدِ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٧٣)
```

```english
And [they say,] ‘Do not believe anyone except him who follows your religion.’ Say, ‘Indeed [true] guidance is the guidance of Allah.’ [They also say.] ‘[Do not believe] that anyone may be given the like of what you were given, or that he may argue with you before your Lord.’ Say, ‘Indeed all grace is in Allah’s hand; He grants it to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (73)
```

```arabic
يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٧٤)
```

```english
He singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes, and Allah is dispenser of a great grace.’ (74)
```

```arabic
۞ وَمِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَنْ إِن تَأْمَنْهُ بِقِنطَارٍ يُؤَدِّهِۦٓ إِلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ إِن تَأْمَنْهُ بِدِينَارٍ لَّا يُؤَدِّهِۦٓ إِلَيْكَ إِلَّا مَا دُمْتَ عَلَيْهِ قَآئِمًا ۗ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لَيْسَ عَلَيْنَا فِى ٱلْأُمِّيِّـۧنَ سَبِيلٌ وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (٧٥)
```

```english
Among the People of the Book is he who if you entrust him with a quintal will repay it to you, and among them is he who, if you entrust him with a dinar will not repay it to you unless you stand persistently over him. That is because they say, ‘We have no obligation to the non-Jews.’ But they attribute lies to Allah, and they know [it]. (75)
```

```arabic
بَلَىٰ مَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِعَهْدِهِۦ وَٱتَّقَىٰ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٧٦)
```

```english
Yes, whoever fulfills his commitments and is wary of Allah—Allah indeed loves the Godwary. (76)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَشْتَرُونَ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَا خَلَـٰقَ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَلَا يُكَلِّمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَا يَنظُرُ إِلَيْهِمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَلَا يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٧٧)
```

```english
There shall be no share in the Hereafter for those who sell Allah’s covenant and their oaths for a paltry gain, and on the Day of Resurrection Allah will not speak to them nor will He [so much as] look at them, nor will He purify them, and there is a painful punishment for them. (77)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ مِنْهُمْ لَفَرِيقًا يَلْوُۥنَ أَلْسِنَتَهُم بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِتَحْسَبُوهُ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَمَا هُوَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (٧٨)
```

```english
There is a group of them who alter their voice while reading out a text [that they have themselves authored], so that you may suppose it to be from the Book, though it is not from the Book, and they say, ‘It is from Allah,’ though it is not from Allah, and they attribute lies to Allah, and they know [it]. (78)
```

## (Commentary)

This marks the beginning of the second stage of the exposition concerning the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) in general, and the Christians (النصارى) in particular, along with related matters. The previous verses addressed the state of the People of the Book in general through His saying: **إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِندَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلَامُ** — "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam" (3:19), and His saying: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيبًا مِّنَ الْكِتَابِ** — "Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book?" (3:23). Then, the discourse shifted specifically to the affair of the Christians with His saying: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفَىٰ آدَمَ وَنُوحًا** — "Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah..." (3:33). In the midst of this, it addressed the matter of the believers' alliance with the disbelievers in His saying: **لَّا يَتَّخِذِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الْكَافِرِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ** — "The faithful should not take the faithless for allies" (3:28). This was the initial stage.

Subsequently, the discourse returns to explaining what was previously mentioned but in a different manner and arrangement. It addresses the state of the People of the Book in general in these recently cited verses, and the miscellaneous matters that will follow according to the specific requirements of the exposition, such as His saying: **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you deny the signs of Allah...’" (3:98), and His saying: **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you bar from the way of Allah...’" (3:99). It also addresses the state of the Christians and their claims regarding the matter of Jesus (عليه السلام) in His saying: **مَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُؤْتِيَهُ اللَّهُ الْكِتَابَ** — "It does not behoove any human that Allah should give him the Book..." (3:79). Furthermore, it addresses matters pertaining to the believers, such as calling them to Islam, unity, and cautioning against the alliance with disbelievers or taking intimates from other than the believers, across many scattered verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قل يا أهل الكتاب تعالوا إلی كلمة سواء بيننا وبينكم** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you’": The address is to the People of the Book in general. The invitation in His saying, "Come to a word..." is, in reality, an invitation to unite upon the meaning of that word through action. It is attributed to the "word" (الكلمة) to indicate that it is something current upon their tongues, similar to our saying, "The word of the people agreed upon such-and-such," which conveys the meaning of submission, acknowledgment, dissemination, and widespread recognition. Thus, the meaning is: Come, let us adopt this word, cooperating and supporting one another in its dissemination and in acting according to its requirements.

The term "common" (السواء) is originally a verbal noun (مصدر), used as an adjective meaning "equal between two sides." "Common between us and you" (سواء بيننا وبينكم) means equal in terms of adopting it and acting upon its requirements. Accordingly, describing the "word" as "common" is a description of the state of its related object, which is the adoption and the action. You have already realized that action pertains to the meaning of the word, not the word itself, just as linking the "union" to the meaning is not devoid of a metaphorical consideration (عناية مجازية). Thus, in the speech, there are several subtle rhetorical considerations: attributing the union to the meaning, then placing the "word" in the place of the "meaning," and then describing the "word" as "common"!

It has been said that the meaning of the word being "common" is that the Quran, the Torah, and the Gospel are in agreement in calling toward it, and it is the word of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*. If this were the intended meaning, then His saying, "that we shall worship none but Allah..." would be a case of placing the true interpretation in the place of the agreed-upon word, while turning away from the displeasing interpretations they played with, which made the word conform to their whims—such as incarnation (الحلول), taking a son, the Trinity (التثليث), and the worship of rabbis, priests, and bishops. The sum of the meaning would then be: Come to a word that is common between us and you, which is monotheism, and the necessary consequence of monotheism is the rejection of partners and not taking lords besides Allah, the Glorified.

That which concludes the verse, His saying: **فإن تولوا فقولوا اشهدوا بأنا مسلمون** — "But if they turn away, say, ‘Be witnesses that we are Muslims’" (3:64), supports the first meaning. The sum of the meaning in light of this is that He calls to this word, which is "that we shall worship none but Allah...", because it is the requirement of Islam (الإسلام) — *the total submission to Allah* — which is the religion (الدين) — *the divine system of belief and practice* — with Allah. Even though Islam is also a necessary consequence of monotheism, the invitation in the verse is specifically toward practical monotheism (التوحيد العملي), which is the abandonment of worshipping other than Allah, the Glorified, rather than just the belief in oneness. Understand this well.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أن لا نعبد إلا الله ولا نشرك به شيئاً ولا يتخذ بعضنا بعضاً أرباباً من دون الله** — "that we shall worship none but Allah, and that we shall not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us shall take others as lords besides Allah": This is an interpretation (تفسير) of the "common word," which is what Islam to Allah necessitates.

The intent of His saying, "that we shall worship none but Allah," is the negation of worshipping other than Allah, not the affirmation of worshipping Allah, the Exalted, as previously indicated regarding the meaning of the word of sincerity (كلمة الإخلاص): "There is no god but Allah" (لا إله إلا الله). The necessity of "but Allah" (إلا الله) being a substitute (بدل) rather than an exception (استثناء) is that the speech is directed toward the negation of a partner (نفي الشريك) rather than the affirmation of the Deity (إثبات الإله), for the Quran takes the affirmation of the Deity's existence and His reality as a given.

Since the speech was directed toward negating a partner in worship, and this does not entirely sever the root of polytheism (الشرك) — *the act of ascribing partners to Allah* — that arises from the belief in sonship, the Trinity, and the like, He followed it with His saying: "and that we shall not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us shall take..." For naming worship as "worship of Allah" does not make it truly worship of Allah, the Glorified, as long as the belief is not sincere and the conscience is not stripped of beliefs and views born from the root of polytheism. In such a case, the worship would merely be the worship of a deity who has a partner. Worship directed toward one of two partners—even if specified by His name and directed toward Him—is nothing but a product of the ground of polytheism, for it is nothing more than a portion assigned to Him and a share divided for Him among two or more partners; thus, it inherently contains a form of worshipping another.

This is what the Prophet calls to by the command of Allah, the Glorified, and it is what is indicated by His saying: "that we shall worship none but Allah, and that we shall not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us shall take others as lords besides Allah." This encapsulates the objective of prophethood (النبوة) — *the divine office of receiving and conveying revelation* — in the way of life (السيرة) that the prophets used to call to and spread within human society.

It was previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying: **كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً** — "Mankind were a single community..." (2:213), that prophethood is a divine mission and a true movement intended to spread the word of religion, and that the reality of religion is the rectification of human society in its vital course. This is followed by the rectification of the life of the individual human, whereby everyone is placed in the position assigned to them by the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and creation. Through this, society is granted the gift of freedom and the felicity of primordial perfection based on justice (العدل) and equity (القسط). Likewise, the individual is absolutely free therein to benefit from the aspects of life in whatever his thought and will guide him to, except for what harms the life of society. All of this is restricted by servitude (العبودية) and Islam to Allah, the Glorified, and submission to the dominance and authority of the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge*.

The summary of this is that what the assembly of prophets (عليه السلام) invited to was for the human species—individually and collectively—to proceed according to what their primordial nature dictates regarding the word of monotheism. This necessitates the application of individual and social actions to Islam toward Allah, and the spreading of equity and justice—meaning the spreading of equality in the rights of life, and freedom in righteous will and righteous deeds (الصالحات).

This cannot be achieved except by severing the roots of discord, wrongful transgression (البغي), the use of force, the enslavement of the weak by the strong and his domination over him, and the weak's worship of the strong. Thus, there is no god but Allah, no lord but Allah, and no judgment (الحكم) except for Allah, the Glorified.

This is what the verse indicates: "that we shall worship none but Allah, and that we shall not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us shall take others as lords besides Allah." And the Exalted said, narrating from Joseph (عليه السلام): **يَا صَاحِبَيِ السِّجْنِ أَأَرْبَابٌ مُّتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ اللَّهُ الْوَاحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِهِ إِلَّا أَسْمَاءً سَمَّيْتُمُوهَا أَنتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُم مَّا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَانٍ إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ** — "O my two prison mates! Are different lords better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount? You do not worship besides Him but names that you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority. Sovereignty belongs only to Allah; He has commanded you to worship none except Him. That is the upright religion" (12:39-40). And the Exalted said: **اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبَارَهُمْ وَرُهْبَانَهُمْ أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ وَالْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَمَا أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا إِلَٰهًا وَاحِدًا لَّا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** — "They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah, and also the Messiah, son of Mary; though they were not commanded except to worship the One God, there is no god except Him" (9:31), among other verses.

In what the Quran narrates regarding the previous prophets such as Noah, Hud, Salih, Abraham, Shu'ayb, Moses, and Jesus (عليه السلام) in their addresses to their nations, there is much of this kind. For instance, the saying of Noah: **قَالَ نُوحٌ رَّبِّ إِنَّهُمْ عَصَوْنِي وَاتَّبَعُوا مَن لَّمْ يَزِدْهُ مَالُهُ وَوَلَدُهُ إِلَّا خَسَارًا** — "Noah said, ‘My Lord! They have disobeyed me, and followed someone whose wealth and children only add to his loss’" (71:21). And the saying of Hud to his people: **أَتَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ آيَةً تَعْبَثُونَ وَتَتَّخِذُونَ مَصَانِعَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ وَإِذَا بَطَشْتُم بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ** — "Do you build a monument on every high ground for vain sport? And do you take up strongholds as if you were to live forever? And when you seize [someone], you seize [him] like tyrants" (26:128-130). And the saying of Salih to his people: **وَلَا تُطِيعُوا أَمْرَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ** — "And do not obey the bid of the profligate" (26:151). And the saying of Abraham to his father and his people: **مَا هَٰذِهِ التَّمَاثِيلُ الَّتِي أَنتُمْ لَهَا عَاكِفُونَ قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا لَهَا عَابِدِينَ قَالَ لَقَدْ كُنتُمْ أَنتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُمْ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ** — "‘What are these images to which you keep clinging?’ They said, ‘We found our fathers worshipping them.’ He said, ‘Certainly you and your fathers have been in manifest error’" (21:52-54). And the saying of the Exalted to Moses and his brother: **اذْهَبَا إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ — إلى أن قال — فَأْتِيَاهُ فَقُولَا إِنَّا رَسُولَا رَبِّكَ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَلَا تُعَذِّبْهُمْ** — "‘Go both of you to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled...’ — until He said — ‘So approach him and say, ‘‘We are the apostles of your Lord. So let the Children of Israel go with us, and do not torture them’’’" (20:43-47). And the saying of Jesus to his people: **وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ** — "...and to clarify for you some of the things about which you differ. So be wary of Allah and obey me" (43:63). Thus, the primordial religion (الدين الفطري) is that which negates transgression, corruption, and these wrongful injustices and authorities that destroy the foundation of felicity and demolish the structure of truth and reality. To this, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) alluded during the Farewell Pilgrimage (as mentioned by al-Mas'udi in the events of the tenth year of the Hijrah in *Muruj al-Dhahab*): **ألا وإن الزمان قد استدار كهيئته يوم خلق الله السموات والأرض** — "Behold, time has turned back to its original state as it was on the day Allah created the heavens and the earth." It is as if he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) intended by this the return of the people to the rule of primordial nature through the establishment of the way of Islam among them.

The speech—meaning His saying, the Exalted: "that we shall worship none but Allah..."—while encapsulating the objective of prophethood, also discloses the reason and the underlying rationale (الملاك) for the ruling.

As for His saying: "that we shall worship none but Allah, and that we shall not ascribe any partner to Him," it is because Divinity (الألوهية) is that toward which everything turns in awe and devotion from every aspect. It means being the Originator of every perfection in things despite their multiplicity, interconnectedness, and unity in need, and in Him is every perfection that things lack. This meaning cannot be sound unless He is One, not many, and the Owner to whom the governance (تدبير) of everything belongs. Therefore, it is obligatory that Allah be worshipped because He is one God with no partner, and it is obligatory that no partner be taken for Him in His worship. In other words, this world and all it contains cannot and must not submit or humble itself except before a single Station; for these subjects (المربوبون), due to the unity of their system and the interconnectedness of their existence, have no lord but one, just as they have no creator but one.

As for His saying, the Exalted: "and that none of us shall take others as lords besides Allah," it is from the perspective that it conveys that human society—despite the multiplicity of its individuals and the separation of its persons—consists of parts (أبعاض) of a single reality, which is the reality of the human being and the species. What the hand of craftsmanship and creation has deposited in it of entitlement and potential, distributed equally among them, necessitates their equality in the rights of life and their standing on a single level. The differences in the states of individuals and their potentials in acquiring the advantages of life from the general human gifts—which appear in specific manifestations here and there—must be granted to humanity, but from the perspective that it seeks them. For example, marriage, childbirth, and medical treatment are matters of general humanity, but the one who is granted marriage is the adult human, male or female; childbirth is granted to the female human; and treatment is granted to the sick human.

In short, the individuals of human society are similar parts of a single, similar reality. Thus, one part should not impose its will and whim upon another unless it bears an equivalent burden, which is cooperation in acquiring the advantages of life. As for the submission of society or an individual to an individual—meaning the whole or a part to a part—in a way that removes it from its "part-hood" and elevates it above equality through superiority, dominance, and control, such that he is taken as a lord whose will is followed, who rules with an unbridled rein, and who is obeyed in what he commands and forbids—therein lies the nullification of primordial nature and the destruction of the human structure.

Furthermore, since Lordship (الربوبية) — *the attribute of creating, sustaining, and governing* — is exclusive to Allah, and there is no lord besides Him, a human being's enabling another like himself to have disposal over him as he wishes without reciprocity is the taking of a lord besides Allah. No one who submits to Allah's command would venture to do such a thing.

Thus, it has become clear that His saying: "and that none of us shall take others as lords besides Allah" discloses two proofs for the meaning it conveys: one is the fact that individuals are parts, and the other is that Lordship is among the exclusive properties of Divinity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فإن تولوا فقولوا اشهدوا بأنا مسلمون** — "But if they turn away, say, ‘Be witnesses that we are Muslims’": This is a call for witnessing that they (the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and those who follow him) are upon the religion pleasing to Allah, the Exalted, which is Islam. He said: **إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِندَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلَامُ** — "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam" (3:19). Thereby, their contention and argumentation are severed, for there is no argument against the truth and its people.

In this, there is an indication that monotheism in worship is among the necessary consequences of Islam.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أهل الكتاب لم تحاجون في إبراهيم** — "O People of the Book! Why do you argue concerning Abraham..." (3:65) until the end of the verse: It appears that this is part of the speech mentioned in the previous verse, as is what follows four verses later. Thus, it is spoken by the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), even though the apparent context of His saying two verses later: **إن أولی الناس بإبراهيم للذين اتبعوه وهذا النبي والذين آمنوا** — "Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this prophet and those who believe..." (3:68) suggests that the address is from Allah, not from His Messenger except by His permission.

Their arguing concerning Abraham (عليه السلام) by each group claiming him for itself seems to have initially been an argument to demonstrate their being on the right path. For instance, the Jews might say: "Abraham (عليه السلام), whom Allah praised in His Book, is from us," and the Christians would say: "Abraham was upon the truth, and the truth appeared with the appearance of Jesus with him." Then it turned into obstinacy and partisanship, with the Jews claiming he was a Jew and the Christians claiming he was a Christian. It is well known that Judaism and Christianity both emerged after the revelation of the Torah and the Gospel, and both were revealed after Abraham (عليه السلام). How then could he (عليه السلام) be a Jew in the sense of adhering to the religion specific to Moses (عليه السلام), or a Christian in the sense of worshipping according to the law of Jesus (عليه السلام)? If anything were to be said about Abraham, it must be said that he was upon the truth, a monotheist (حنيفاً) — *one who turns away from all false religions toward the true one* — turning from falsehood to truth, submitting to Allah, the Glorified. These verses follow the course of His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ تَقُولُونَ إِنَّ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْمَاعِيلَ وَإِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَالْأَسْبَاطَ كَانُوا هُودًا أَوْ نَصَارَىٰ ، قُلْ أَأَنتُمْ أَعْلَمُ أَمِ اللَّهُ وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَتَمَ شَهَادَةً عِندَهُ مِنَ اللَّهِ** — "Do you say that Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the Tribes were Jews or Christians? Say, ‘Do you know better, or Allah? And who is a greater wrongdoer than him who conceals a testimony he has from Allah?’" (2:140).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ها أنتم حاججتم فيما لكم به علم فلم تحاجون فيما ليس لكم به علم** — "Ah! You are the ones who argued about that of which you had some knowledge. Why then do you argue about that of which you have no knowledge?" (3:66): The verse affirms for them some knowledge in the argument that took place between them, negates some knowledge, and affirms it for Allah, the Exalted. Therefore, the commentators mentioned that the meaning is: You argued concerning Abraham (عليه السلام) while having some knowledge of him, such as knowledge of his existence and prophethood; why then do you argue about that of which you have no knowledge, which is his being a Jew or a Christian, while Allah knows and you do not know? Or, that the intended knowledge is the knowledge of Jesus and his news; the meaning being that you argue concerning Jesus while you have knowledge of his news, so why do you argue about that of which you have no knowledge, which is Abraham being a Jew or a Christian? This is what they mentioned.

You know that neither of these two views aligns with the apparent context of the verse. As for the first, it is because no argument occurred among them regarding the existence of Abraham or his prophethood. As for the second, it is because the argument they had concerning Jesus was not correct; rather, they were mistaken in his news and lying in their claims about him. How then could it be called an argument concerning that of which they have knowledge? In any case, His speech affirms an argument from them concerning that of which they have knowledge, just as it affirms an argument concerning that of which they have no knowledge. So what is this argument that is concerning what they have knowledge of? Furthermore, the apparent meaning of the verse is that both of these arguments took place among the People of the Book themselves, not between them and the Muslims; otherwise, the Muslims would be upon falsehood in the argument where the People of the Book have knowledge, and this is obvious.

What ought to be said—and Allah knows best—is that it is known that the argument was ongoing between the Jews and the Christians in all matters of difference between them. The core of that was the prophethood of Jesus (عليه السلام) and what the Christians said regarding him (that he is God, or His son, or the Trinity). The Christians would argue with the Jews regarding his mission and prophethood, and they had knowledge of it; and the Jews would argue with the Christians, nullifying his divinity, his prophethood, and the Trinity, and they had knowledge of it. This was their argument concerning that of which they had knowledge. As for their argument concerning that of which they had no knowledge, it was their argument regarding the matter of Abraham—that he was a Jew or a Christian.

The intent of their ignorance of it is not their ignorance of the revelation of the Torah and the Gospel after him, for that is obvious. Nor is it their oversight of the fact that the predecessor cannot be a follower of the successor, for that contradicts what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **أفلا تعقلون** — "Do you not exercise your reason?" (3:65), as it indicates that the matter requires only the slightest reminder. Thus, they know that he preceded the Torah and the Gospel, but they are heedless of the requirement of their knowledge, which is that he could not then be a Jew or a Christian, but rather upon the religion of Allah, which is Islam to Allah.

However, the Jews nonetheless said: "The true religion can only be one, and it is Judaism; therefore, Abraham was inevitably a Jew." The Christians said the same and claimed Abraham for Christianity. In this, they were ignorant of a matter, and it was not mere heedlessness: that the religion of Allah is one, and it is Islam to Allah. It is one, becoming complete according to the passage of time and the potential of the people in terms of their gradual progression toward perfection. Judaism and Christianity are two branches among the branches of the perfection of Islam, which is the root of the religion. The prophets (عليه السلام) are like the builders of this structure; each of them has his place in what he laid of the foundation and what he built upon this lofty structure.

In short, the Jews and the Christians were ignorant of the fact that Abraham being the founder of Islam—which is the original, true religion—and then the appearance of a true religion named Judaism or Christianity—which is the name of a branch of its perfection and stages of its completion—does not necessitate that Abraham be a Jew or a Christian. Rather, he would be a monotheist Muslim, characterized by the name of Islam which he founded, and which is the root of Judaism and Christianity, not the other way around. The root is not attributed to its branch; rather, the branch should be traced back to it.

Naming Abraham a Muslim, and not a Jew or a Christian, is not the same as counting him a follower of the religion of the Prophet and the law of the Quran, such that the objection would arise: just as he preceded the revelation of the Torah and the Gospel and thus should not be counted a Jew or a Christian, he also preceded the revelation of the Quran and the appearance of Islam and thus should not be counted a Muslim (following the same logic).

This is because Islam in the sense of the law of the Quran is among the technical terms that emerged after the revelation of the Quran and the spread of the fame of the Muhammadan religion. The Islam with which Abraham was described is the root of submission (التسليم) to Allah, the Glorified, and humbling oneself before the station of His Lordship. Thus, the objection does not arise from its root.

Perhaps this which we mentioned—regarding the aspect of their ignorance of the meaning of the original religion and its being a reality with different stages progressing in completion—is what is intended by His saying, the Exalted: **والله يعلم وأنتم لا تعلمون ما كان إبراهيم يهودياً** — "And Allah knows and you do not know. Abraham was not a Jew..." (3:66-67). This is supported by His saying: **إن أولی الناس بإبراهيم للذين اتبعوه** — "Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who followed him..." (3:68), and His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verses: **قُلْ آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ وَمَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا وَمَا أُنزِلَ عَلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْمَاعِيلَ وَإِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَالْأَسْبَاطِ وَمَا أُوتِيَ مُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَالنَّبِيُّونَ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُ مُسْلِمُونَ وَمَن يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ الْإِسْلَامِ دِينًا فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ** — "Say, ‘We believe in Allah and what has been sent down to us, and what was sent down to Abraham and Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob and the Tribes, and what was given to Moses and Jesus and to the prophets from their Lord. We make no distinction between any of them, and to Him do we submit.’ Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam, it shall never be accepted from him..." (3:84-85), as will come in the exposition.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما كان إبراهيم يهودياً ولا نصرانياً** — "Abraham was not a Jew nor a Christian..." (3:67) until the end of the verse: Its interpretation has already passed. It has been said that just as the Jews and Christians claimed that Abraham (عليه السلام) was from them and upon their religion, the pagan Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) also claimed that they were upon the monotheistic religion (الدين الحنيف), the religion of Abraham (عليه السلام), to the extent that the People of the Book used to call them "the monotheists" (الحنفاء) and would call paganism "monotheism" (الحنيفية).

When Allah, the Glorified, described Abraham (عليه السلام) by His saying: "but he was a monotheist" (ولكن كان حنيفاً), it was necessary to clarify it so that paganism would not be imagined from it. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: "a Muslim, and he was not one of the polytheists" (مسلماً وما كان من المشركين), meaning he was upon the religion pleasing to Allah, which is Islam, and he was not among the polytheists like the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إن أولی الناس بإبراهيم للذين اتبعوه وهذا النبي والذين آمنوا** — "Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this prophet and those who believe..." (3:68): This verse is in the position of providing the reason for the previous speech and a clarification of the truth in this matter. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that if the relationship were taken between this noble prophet, Abraham, and those who came after him of the adherents and others, the truth would be that he should not be considered a follower of those who came after him. Rather, the priority (الأولوية) and proximity to him should be considered. The nearest to a prophet who has a law and a book are those who share with him in following the truth and adhering to the religion he brought. The nearest in this sense to Abraham (عليه السلام) are this Prophet and those who believe, because they are upon the Islam for which Allah chose Abraham, as is everyone who followed him, excluding those who disbelieve in the signs of Allah and shroud the truth with falsehood.

In His saying: "those who followed him" (للذين اتبعوه), there is an indirect critique of the People of the Book—the Jews and the Christians—by way of allusion; meaning: you are not the nearest to Abraham because of your failure to follow him in his Islam to Allah.

In His saying: "and this prophet and those who believe" (وهذا النبي والذين آمنوا), there is a singling out of the Prophet (عليه السلام) and those who followed him of the believers from those who followed Abraham, out of honor for the Prophet and to protect his station from having "following" (الاتباع) applied to him directly, as is sensed from similar verses like His saying, the Exalted: **أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللَّهُ فَبِهُدَاهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ** — "They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance" (6:90), where He did not say: "So follow them."

He completed the reasoning and clarification with His saying: **والله ولي المؤمنين** — "and Allah is the guardian of the faithful" (3:68). For the guardianship (ولاية) of Abraham (the friend of Allah) is from the guardianship of Allah, and Allah is the guardian of the believers, not others who disbelieve in His signs and shroud the truth with falsehood.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ودت طائفة من أهل الكتاب لو يضلونكم وما يضلون إلا أنفسهم وما يشعرون** — "A group of the People of the Book would love to lead you astray; but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they are not aware" (3:69): A "group" (الطائفة) is a body of people. It seems the origin of the term is that people—especially the Arabs—initially lived as nomadic tribes and clans, wandering in summer and winter with their livestock in search of water and pasture. They would wander as a group (جماعة) to guard against assassination and raids; thus, a group was called a "circling body" (طائفة). Later, the description "group" (الطائفة) was used alone to denote a body of people.

As for the fact that the People of the Book mislead none but themselves, it is because the foremost of human virtues is the inclination toward the truth and following it. Thus, the desire to turn people away from the truth toward falsehood—from the perspective that it is one of the states and characters of the soul—is a psychological vice (رذيلة نفسانية)—and what a wretched vice it is—and one of its sins, transgressions, and wrongful outrages. What is there after the truth except misguidance? Thus, their love for misleading the believers while they are upon the truth is itself a misguidance for their own souls, though they are not aware.

Likewise, if they were able to mislead some of them by casting doubts, they would first be misleading themselves. This is because a human being does nothing of good or evil except for himself, as the Exalted said: **مَّنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ أَسَاءَ فَعَلَيْهَا وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ** — "Whoever acts righteously, it is for his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its detriment, and your Lord is not tyrannical to [His] servants" (41:46). As for the misguidance of the one who is misled by their misleading, it is not due to an effect from them; rather, it is due to the evil deeds of the misguided one and the ill-omen of his will, by permission from Allah. The Exalted said: **مَن كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَلِأَنفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ** — "Whoever disbelieves, his disbelief shall be to his own detriment, and those who act righteously, they prepare for their own souls" (30:44). And the Exalted said: **وَمَا أَصَابَكُم مِّن مُّصِيبَةٍ فَبِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُو عَن كَثِيرٍ وَمَا أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ مِن وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ** — "Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]. You cannot frustrate [Him] on the earth, and you do not have any guardian or helper besides Allah" (42:30-31). A portion of the discourse on the properties of deeds has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ** — "...their works have failed in this world and the Hereafter" (2:217), in the second volume of the book.

This which we have mentioned is among the Quranic gnoses (المعارف) provided by active monotheism (التوحيد الأفعالي), which branches out from the all-encompassing rule of Lordship and Ownership. Through this, the restriction (الحصر) conveyed by His saying, the Exalted: "but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they are not aware," is explained.

As for the explanations mentioned by the commentators for the meaning of the verse, they do not provide anything substantial regarding the mentioned restriction; therefore, we have refrained from citing them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أهل الكتاب لم تكفرون بآيات الله وأنتم تشهدون** — "O People of the Book! Why do you deny the signs of Allah while you bear witness?" (3:70): It has already passed that disbelief in the signs of Allah is different from disbelief in Allah, the Exalted. Disbelief in Allah is the explicit commitment to the negation of monotheism, such as paganism and atheism (الدهرية). Disbelief in the signs of Allah is the denial of any of the divine gnoses after the clarification has arrived and the truth has become clear. The People of the Book do not deny that the world has one God; rather, they deny matters of the truths clarified for them by the heavenly Books revealed to them and to others—such as the prophethood of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), Jesus being a servant of Allah and a messenger from Him, Abraham being neither a Jew nor a Christian, that Allah's hand is outstretched, and that Allah is All-sufficient (غني); among others. Thus, the People of the Book, in the language of the Quran, are "disbelievers in the signs of Allah" (كافرون بآيات الله), not "disbelievers in Allah." This is not contradicted by His saying, the Exalted: **قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَلَا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَلَا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ الْحَقِّ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ** — "Fight those who do not believe in Allah nor in the Last Day, and who do not forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, and who do not congratulate the true religion, from among those who were given the Book..." (9:29), where He explicitly negated faith from them, which is nothing but disbelief. This is because the mention of their not forbidding the forbidden and not following the true religion in the verse testifies that the intent of describing them as lacking faith is a description by the necessary consequence of their state. The necessary consequence of their state of disbelief in the signs of Allah is the lack of faith in Allah and the Last Day, even if they are not aware of it; it is not explicit disbelief.

In His saying, the Exalted: "while you bear witness" (وأنتم تشهدون)—and witnessing (الشهادة) is presence and knowledge derived from the senses—there is an indication that the intent of their disbelief in the signs of Allah is their denial that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is the promised Prophet of whom the Torah and the Gospel gave glad tidings, despite their witnessing the correspondence of the signs and marks mentioned in them to him.

From here, the invalidity of what some have mentioned becomes apparent: that the word "signs" (الآيات) is general, encompassing all signs, and there is no reason to restrict it to the signs of prophethood; rather, the intent is their disbelief in all true signs. The reason for its invalidity is obvious.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أهل الكتاب لم تلبسون الحق بالباطل** — "O People of the Book! Why do you confound the truth with falsehood..." (3:71) until the end of the verse: "Confounding" (اللبس) with a *fathah* on the *lam* means casting doubt and camouflaging; that is, you present the truth in the form of falsehood.

In His saying: "while you know" (وأنتم تعلمون), there is an indication or an allusion that the intent of confounding and concealment is what pertains to religious gnoses other than the witnessed signs—such as the verses they distorted, concealed, or interpreted in a way other than what was intended.

These two verses—meaning His saying: "O People of the Book! Why do you deny..." until His saying: "...while you know"—are a completion of His saying, the Exalted: "A group... would love..." (3:69). Accordingly, the reprimand of the whole for the actions of the few by attributing it to them is due to their unity in element, lineage, and character, and the approval of some for the actions of others, which is frequently occurring in the Quran.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وقالت طائفة من أهل الكتاب آمنوا بالذي أُنزل** — "A group of the People of the Book says, ‘Believe in what has been sent down...’" (3:72) until the end of the verse: The intent of "the face of the day" (وجه النهار), by the context of its contrast with "its end," is its beginning. For the "face" of a thing is what appears and shows it to others, and in the day, it is its beginning. The context of their saying reveals the revelation of a message to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) at the beginning of the day that agreed with what the People of the Book held, and another at its end that contradicted what they held; this is what prompted them to say this.

Accordingly, His saying: "in what has been sent down to those who believe" (بالذي أُنزل على الذين آمنوا) intended a specific part of the Quranic revelation that agreed with what the People of the Book had. His saying: "at the beginning of the day" (وجه النهار) is in the accusative case as an adverb of time (ظرفية) and is related to His saying: "has been sent down" (أُنزل), not to His saying: "Believe" (آمنوا) (the imperative form), because it is closer. His saying: "and disbelieve at its end" (واكفروا آخره) is in the sense of "and disbelieve in what was sent down at its end." Thus, it is a case of placing the container (the time) in the place of the contained (the revelation) by way of intellectual metaphor (المجاز العقلي), similar to His saying, the Exalted: **بَلْ مَكْرُ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهَارِ** — "Rather, [it was your] plotting by night and day" (34:33).

By this, what has been narrated regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt is supported: that this was a word spoken by the Jews at the time of the change of the *qiblah* (القبلة) — *the direction faced during prayer*. The Messenger of Allah prayed the dawn prayer toward Jerusalem, which was the *qiblah* of the Jews, then the *qiblah* was changed during the noon prayer toward the Kaaba. So a group of the Jews said: "Believe in what has been sent down to those who believe at the beginning of the day"—intending the facing of Jerusalem—"and disbelieve at its end"—intending the facing of the Kaaba. This is supported by their subsequent saying, as narrated by Allah: **ولا تؤمنوا لا لمن تبع دينكم** — "and do not believe anyone except him who follows your religion" (3:73); meaning: do not trust anyone who does not follow your religion by believing in him, such that you disclose to him any of your secrets and the glad tidings you possess. Among the signs of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was that he would change the *qiblah* to the Kaaba.

Some have mentioned that His saying: "at the beginning of the day" is related to His saying: "Believe" (in the imperative form), meaning the first part of the day. His saying: "its end" is an adverb with the preposition "in" (في) implied, and is related to His saying: "and disbelieve." The intent of their saying: "Believe in what has been sent down..." was for a number of them to manifest belief in the Quran and join the community of believers, then apostatize at the end of the day by manifesting that they only believed at the beginning of the day because of the signs of truthfulness and truth that seemed to appear to them from the outward Islamic call, and they only apostatized at the end of the day because of the evidence of falsehood that became clear to them and the lack of correspondence of the glad tidings of prophethood and signs of truth they possessed with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). This would be a plot to deceive the believers so they would doubt their religion and weaken in their resolve, thereby breaking their momentum and nullifying their cause.

This meaning is not far-fetched in itself, especially from the Jews who spared no effort in attacking Islam to extinguish its light by any possible means. However, the wording of the verse does not align with it. A completion of the discourse will follow, which we will address in the subsequent discussion of narrations, if Allah, the Mighty, wills.

Others said: The intent is "Believe in your prayer toward the Kaaba at the beginning of the day and disbelieve in it at its end, so that they may return." Others said: The meaning is "Manifest belief at the beginning of the day in what you acknowledged of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) description, and disbelieve at its end by showing that what the promised Prophet was described with does not apply to him, so that they may doubt thereby and return from their religion." These two views have no supporting evidence. Regardless of the intended meaning, there is no ambiguity in the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ولا تؤمنوا إلا لمن تبع دينكم** — "and do not believe anyone except him who follows your religion" (3:73): What the context provides is that this sentence is from the speech of the People of the Book, a completion of their saying: "Believe in what has been sent down to those who believe." Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: **أن يؤتي أحد مثل ما اوتيتم أو يحاجوكم به عند ربكم** — "that anyone should be given the like of what you were given, or that they should argue with you before your Lord." His saying: **قل إن الهدی هدی الله** — "Say, ‘Indeed [true] guidance is the guidance of Allah’" is a parenthetical sentence (جملة معترضة) which is Allah's response to the sum of what preceded of their speech—meaning their saying: "Believe in what was sent down..." until His saying: "...your religion"—as indicated by the change in context. Similarly, His saying, the Exalted: **قل إن الفضل بيد الله** — "Say, ‘Indeed grace is in the hand of Allah’" is His response to their saying: "that anyone should be given..." until the end. This is what is necessitated by the connection of the parts of the speech and the consistency of the meanings in the two verses, and by the similarity of these two verses to the verses narrating the sayings of the Jews in argumentation and plotting.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that a group of the People of the Book—the Jews—said, meaning some said to others: "Confirm the Prophet and the believers in their prayer at the beginning of the day toward Jerusalem, and do not confirm them in their prayer toward the Kaaba at the end of the day. And do not trust in speaking to others, lest they inform the believers that among the evidences of the promised Prophet's prophethood is the change of the *qiblah* to the Kaaba. For in your confirmation of the matter of the Kaaba and your disclosure of what you know of its being among the signs of the truthfulness of the call, there is the danger that the believers will be given the like of what you were given of the *qiblah*, whereby your prestige would vanish and your precedence in the matter of the *qiblah* would be nullified. And there is the danger that they will establish the proof against you before your Lord—that you were aware of the matter of the new *qiblah*, witnessing its truth, yet you did not believe."

So Allah responded to their saying regarding believing in what is at the beginning of the day and disbelieving at its end, and their command to conceal the matter of the *qiblah* so that the believers would not be guided to the truth, by stating that the guidance the believers need—which is the true guidance—is only the guidance of Allah, not your guidance. Thus, the believers are in no need of that; so if you wish, follow; and if you wish, disbelieve; and if you wish, disclose; and if you wish, conceal.

And the Exalted responded to what they mentioned of the fear that anyone should be given the like of what they were given or that they should argue with them before their Lord, by stating that grace (الفضل) is in the hand of Allah, He gives it to whom He wills, not in your hand such that you could keep it for yourselves and prevent others from it. As for the talk of concealment for fear of argumentation, He turned away from responding to it due to its obvious invalidity, just as He did in His saying, the Exalted, regarding this very meaning: **وَإِذَا لَقُوا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قَالُوا آمَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَا بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ قَالُوا أَتُحَدِّثُونَهُم بِمَا فَتَحَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لِيُحَاجُّوكُم بِهِ عِندَ رَبِّكُمْ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ أَوَلَا يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ** — "When they meet the faithful, they say, ‘We believe,’ and when they are alone with one another, they say, ‘Do you discourse to them of what Allah has disclosed to you, so that they may argue with you before your Lord? Do you not exercise your reason?’ Do they not know that Allah knows whatever they hide and whatever they disclose?" (2:76-77). His saying: "Do they not know..." is an indication that this saying—after they knew that Allah does not differ between the secret and the manifest—is a speech from them that does not stand upon sound reasoning; it is not a response because of the "and" (الواو) in His saying: "Do they not know."

According to the aforementioned meaning, His saying, the Exalted: "and do not believe" (ولا تؤمنوا) means: do not trust and do not confirm for them trust and the keeping of secrets, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "...and he trusts the faithful" (9:61). The intent of His saying: "him who follows" (لمن تبع) is the Jews.

The intent of the sentence is the prohibition of disclosing what they had of the truth of the change of the *qiblah* to the Kaaba, as passed in His saying, the Exalted: **فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ — إلى أن قال : وَإِنَّ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ لَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهم — إلى أن قال : الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ يَعْرِفُونَهُ كَمَا يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْنَاءَهُمْ وَإِنَّ فَرِيقًا مِّنْهُمْ لَيَكْتُمُونَ الْحَقَّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** — "‘...so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque...’ — until He said — ‘Indeed those who were given the Book surely know that it is the truth from their Lord...’ — until He said — ‘Those whom We have given the Book recognize him just as they recognize their sons, but a part of them indeed conceal the truth while they know’" (2:144-146).

Regarding the meaning of the verse, there are various opinions circulating among the commentators. Some say that His saying, the Exalted: "and do not believe..." until the end of the verse is the speech of Allah, not the Jews. The plural address in His saying: "and do not believe" and His saying: "what you were given or that they should argue with you before your Lord" are all for the believers, and the singular address in His saying: "Say" in both places is for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Others say the same, except that the plural address in His saying: "you were given or that they should argue with you before your Lord" is for the Jews as a reprimand and censure. Others say that His saying: "and do not believe anyone except him who follows your religion" is from the speech of the Jews, and His saying: "Say, ‘Indeed [true] guidance is the guidance of Allah,’ that anyone should be given..." is the speech of Allah as a response to what the Jews said. Likewise, there is disagreement regarding the meaning of "grace" (الفضل)—whether it refers to religion, worldly blessing, victory, or something else.

These opinions, despite their multiplicity, are far from what the context provides, as we previously indicated; therefore, we did not occupy ourselves with them further.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قل إن الفضل بيد الله يؤتيه من يشاء والله واسع عليم** — "Say, ‘Indeed grace is in the hand of Allah; He gives it to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing’" (3:73): Grace (الفضل) is that which exceeds moderation. It is used for the praiseworthy, just as "superfluity" (الفضول) is used for the blameworthy. Al-Raghib said: "Every gift that is not obligatory upon the giver is called grace, such as His saying: 'and ask Allah of His grace'—'that is the grace of Allah'—'Possessor of great grace.' Accordingly, His saying: 'Say, by the grace of Allah'—'and were it not for the grace of Allah...'" End quote.

Accordingly, His saying: "Indeed grace is in the hand of Allah" is a form of conciseness by sufficing with the major premise of the syllogistic exposition. The estimation is: Say, indeed this revelation and divine granting—which you plot to restrict to yourselves by feigning belief and disbelief, and by advising the concealment of the matter—is not something we, the assembly of people, deserve from Allah; rather, it is from grace. Grace is in the hand of Allah, to whom belongs the kingdom and the judgment; so He has the right to give it to whom He wills, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing.

In the speech, there is a negation of what their words and actions indicate regarding the restriction of divine blessing to themselves in all its possible aspects. For the blessing of some people with Allah's grace to the exclusion of others—like the Jews being blessed with the religion and the *qiblah* and others being deprived—would either be because grace from Him, the Exalted, can fall under the influence of another, thereby interfering with the divine will, withholding His grace from one side and directing it to another; but it is not so, for grace is in the hand of Allah, He gives it to whom He wills.

Or it would be because grace is little and insufficient, while those to be graced are many; thus, its granting to some to the exclusion of others would require the addition of a preponderant factor (مرجح), so one plots to establish a factor to restrict it to the part that is blessed; but it is not so, for Allah, the Glorified, is all-bounteous (واسع) in grace and power.

Or it would be because grace, though bounteous and in Allah's hand, the one to be graced could be hidden from Him, the Exalted, due to ignorance on His part, so he does not attain the grace; thus, one plots to hide him and veil his state from Him, the Exalted, so he is deprived of His grace; but it is not so, for Allah, the Glorified, is all-knowing (عليم), and no ignorance occurs to Him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يختص برحمته من يشاء والله ذو الفضل العظيم** — "He singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes, and Allah is the dispenser of a great grace" (3:74): Since grace is in the hand of Allah, He gives it to whom He wills, and He is all-bounteous and all-knowing, it is possible for Him to single out some of His servants for some of His blessings. For He has the right to dispose of His kingdom as He wills. It is not the case that if He is not prevented from disposing of His grace and giving it to His servants, He must give all His grace to everyone; for this too would be a kind of restriction in disposal. Rather, He has the right to single out for His grace whom He wills.

He concluded the speech with His saying: "and Allah is the dispenser of a great grace," which is in the position of a reason for all the preceding meanings. For the necessity of the absolute greatness of grace is that it be in His hand, He gives it to whom He wills; that He be all-bounteous in His grace; that He be all-knowing of the state of His servants and what grace is suitable for their state; and that He have the right to single out for His grace whom He wills.

In the substitution of "grace" with "mercy" (الرحمة) in His saying: "He singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes," there is an indication that grace—which is the non-obligatory gift—is a branch of mercy. The Exalted said: **وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ** — "...but My mercy embraces all things" (7:156). And He said: **وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ مَا زَكَىٰ مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَدًا** — "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever be pure" (24:21). And the Exalted said: **قُل لَّوْ أَنتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزَائِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّي إِذًا لَّأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ الْإِنفَاقِ** — "Say, ‘If you possessed the treasuries of my Lord’s mercy, you would surely withhold [them] for fear of spending’" (17:100).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ومن أهل الكتاب من إن تأمنه بقنطار يؤده إليك** — "Among the People of the Book is he who, if you entrust him with a quintal, will restore it to you..." (3:75) until His saying: "...any way [to blame us]": This is an indication of their vast difference in the preservation of trusts and covenants, a difference that reaches the extremes of contradiction. Although this is in itself a harmful national vice, it arose among them and spread in their community from another doctrinal vice, which is what their saying contains: "There is no way [to blame us] concerning the unlettered (الاميين)." For they used to call themselves "the People of the Book" and others "the unlettered." Their saying: "There is no way [to blame us] concerning the unlettered" means the negation of any right for a non-Israelite over an Israelite. They attributed this word to the religion, and the evidence for it is His saying, the Exalted: **ويقولون علی الله الكذب وهم يعلمون بلى** — "...and they tell lies against Allah while they know. Yes..." (3:75-76).

They used to imagine—as they do today in their imagination—that they are the ones specifically chosen for divine honor, which does not pass from them to others, since Allah, the Glorified, placed among them prophethood, a book, and kingship. Thus, they have sovereignty and precedence over others. They concluded from this that the rights legislated among them, which they are required to observe—such as the prohibition of taking usury, consuming the wealth of others, and encroaching upon people's rights—are only among themselves, the assembly of the People of the Book. Thus, what is forbidden is an Israelite consuming the wealth of another like him, and what is prohibited is encroaching upon the rights of a Jew by one of his own faith. In short, the "way" (السبيل) is only for the People of the Book against the People of the Book; as for those other than the People of the Book, they have no "way" against the People of the Book. Thus, they may judge others as they wish and do to those below them as they desire. This leads to treating others as if they were dumb animals, whoever they may be.

Although this is not found in the books they possess that are attributed to revelation, such as the Torah and others, it is a matter they took from the mouths of their rabbis and followed them in it. Then, since the Mosaic religion did not pass beyond the Children of Israel to others, they made it a nationality among them. From this was born the idea that this honor and sovereignty is a national matter specifically for the Children of Israel. Thus, Israelite lineage is the substance of honor and the element of sovereignty, and the one related to Israel has absolute precedence over others. This transgressive spirit, when it creeps into the mold of a people, drives them to corrupt the earth and kill the human spirit and its governing effects in human society.

Yes, the root of this word—which is the stripping of general rights from some individuals and communities—is something unavoidable in human society. However, what the righteous human society considers is the stripping of rights from those who wish to nullify rights and destroy society. What Islam considers in the establishment of rights is the religion of monotheism, whether through Islam or the status of protected minority (الذمة). Thus, he who has neither Islam nor *dhimmah* has no right to life, which corresponds to the primordial law (الناموس الفطري) that you heard is considered in general by human society.

Let us return to our discussion of the verse. His saying, the Exalted: "And among the People of the Book" (ومن أهل الكتاب)—it would have been apparent to say: "And among them" (ومنهم). This is a case of placing the explicit noun in the place of the pronoun, and the reason for it is to prevent the illusion that these are some of the group mentioned in the two previous verses who said: "Believe in what has been sent down..." Therefore, when the mentioned illusion was dispelled, it was said in the following verse: **وإن منهم لفريقاً يلوون ألسنتهم بالكتاب** — "And there is indeed a group of them who twist their tongues with the Book..." (3:78).

There is another perspective, which is that mentioning the description—namely, their being from the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب)—suggests a kind of causal explanation (تعليل). This is because the issuance of such a statement and action from them—meaning their saying: "There is no way against us concerning the unlettered (الاميين)," and their consuming people's wealth based on that—would not have been so far-fetched or strange had they been unlettered (اميين) with no knowledge of prophecy or revelation. However, they are the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), and they possess the Book containing the judgment of Allah (حكم الله), and they know that the Book does not judge in their favor in this regard, nor does it permit them the wealth of others simply because they are "others." Thus, what they said and subsequently did, while being the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), is stranger and more far-fetched coming from them, and the rebuke and condemnation against them are more appropriate and binding.

The *qintar* (القنطار) — *a large weight of gold or wealth* — and the *dinar* (الدينار) — *a single gold coin* — are well-known. The contrast between them—notwithstanding the rhetorical beauty (المحسنات البديعية) it contains—in a context where trust (الأمانة) is mentioned, indicates that He used them as metaphors for a large amount and a small amount. The meaning is that among them are those who do not betray a trust even if it is vast and of heavy value, and among them are those who betray it even if it is meager and light.

Similarly, the address employed in the speech, in His saying: "if you entrust him with a *qintar*, he will pay it back to you," is not directed at a specific addressee. Rather, it is a metaphorical use (تكنية) for any addressee who could potentially be addressed with this speech, to indicate that the ruling is general and not restricted to one person over another. The speech carries the meaning of our saying: "If any trustee (مؤتمن), whoever he may be, entrusts him with a *qintar*, he will pay it back to him."

The particle *ma* (ما) in His saying: "unless you keep standing over him," is a gerundive particle (مصدرية) according to what has been said, and the estimation is: "except for your continuous standing over him." The mention of "standing over him" (القيام عليه) is to indicate persistence and urgency, for the claimant's standing on his feet during the demand without sitting down is evidence of that. It has been suggested that *ma* is adverbial (ظرفية), but that is of no consequence.

Regarding His saying: **ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا لَيْسَ عَلَيْنَا فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ سَبِيلٌ** "That is because they say, ‘We have no obligation toward the gentiles,’" the apparent context is that "that" (ذلك) refers to the collective content derived from the preceding statement. That is, the fact that some of them fulfill the trust even if it is significant and important, while some do not fulfill it even if it is contemptible and disregarded, is only because of their saying: "There is no way against us concerning the unlettered (الاميين)." This necessitated a divergence among them in spiritual qualities, such as preserving trusts and guarding against wasting people's rights, and being deluded by a sense of nobility, despite their knowing that Allah did not legislate that for them in the Book, nor did He approve of such actions from them.

It is possible that "that" (ذلك) refers to the state of the second group mentioned in His saying: "and among them is he who, if you entrust him with a *dinar*, will not pay it back to you." In this case, the mention of the first, trustworthy group would be to complete all categories and to maintain fairness (النصفة). It is then permissible for the plural pronouns in His saying: "and they say" (ويقولون) and in His saying: "and they know" (وهم يعلمون) to refer back to the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), or to refer back to His saying: "he who, if you entrust him with a *dinar*," according to the meaning. Likewise, under the second possibility, it is permissible for the first-person pronoun in His saying: "against us" (علينا) to refer to all the People of the Book or specifically to some of them. The meaning varies according to these possibilities, but all of them are correct and sound; you should reflect upon them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** "and they attribute lies to Allah, and they know [it]" is a refutation of their claim that "there is no way against us concerning the unlettered," and evidence that they used to attribute that to heavenly revelation and religious legislation, as previously mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بَلَىٰ مَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِعَهْدِهِ وَاتَّقَىٰ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ** "Yes, whoever fulfills his pledges and is God-conscious—Allah indeed loves the God-conscious" is a rebuttal of their words and an affirmation of what they denied by their saying: "There is no way against us concerning the unlettered." Fulfilling the pledge (إيفاء العهد) is to complete it by guarding against excuses and deficiency. *Tawfiyah* (التوفية) is to bestow and give in full, and *istifa’* (الاستيفاء) is to take and receive in full.

What is meant by the pledge (العهد) is the covenant (الميثاق) that Allah took from His servants that they should believe in Him and worship Him, as suggested by His saying in the following verse: "Indeed those who sell the covenant of Allah and their oaths for a small price." Or it refers to the absolute pledge (العهد), of which the pledge of Allah (عهد الله) is a part.

His saying: "for Allah indeed loves the God-conscious (المتقين)" is an instance of placing the major premise (الكبرى) in the position of the minor premise (الصغرى) for the sake of conciseness. The estimation is: "for Allah loves him because he is God-conscious (متق), and Allah loves the God-conscious (المتقين)." The meaning is that the honor (الكرامة) from Allah for His God-conscious servants is His love for them, not what you claimed regarding the "negation of the way" (نفي السبيل).

The import of the speech is that divine honor (الكرامة الإلهية) is not something cheap or easily attained such that anyone who has a mere affiliation to Him can reach it, or that every deceiver or arrogant person can consider it a racial or national honor. Rather, the condition for attaining it is the fulfillment of Allah's pledge and covenant, and godwariness (التقوى) in religion. When the conditions are met, the honor is achieved, which is the divine love and guardianship (الولاية الإلهية) that does not bypass His God-conscious servants. Its effect is divine assistance and a felicitous life (الحيوة السعيدة) that prospers the world, rectifies the state of its people, and raises the degrees of the Hereafter.

This is the divine honor; it is not that He should carry one people upon the shoulders of His other servants—righteous and wicked alike—and set them free to do as they wish and act as they please, so they say one day: "There is no way against us concerning the unlettered," and another day: "We are the friends of Allah to the exclusion of all people," and another day: "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones," leading them thereby to spread corruption on earth and destroy crops and progeny.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَشْتَرُونَ بِعَهْدِ اللَّهِ وَأَيْمَانِهِمْ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا** "Indeed those who sell the covenant of Allah and their oaths for a small price" is a causal explanation (تعليل) for the ruling mentioned in the previous verse. The meaning is that divine honor is exclusive to those who fulfill their pledge and are God-conscious, because others—those who sell the covenant of Allah and their oaths for a small price—have no honor.

Since the breaking of Allah's covenant and the abandonment of godwariness (التقوى) are only for the sake of enjoying the vanities of the world and preferring the desires of the first life over the last, it involved placing the enjoyment of the world in the place of fulfilling the pledge and godwariness, and exchanging the pledge for it. Therefore, He likened their action to a transaction, making the covenant of Allah the commodity sold for enjoyment, and He called the enjoyment of the world—which is meager—a "small price" (ثمناً قليلاً). "Buying" (الاشتراء) here is selling (البيع), so it was said: "they buy a small price with the covenant of Allah and their oaths," meaning they exchange the covenant and oaths for the enjoyment of the world.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُولَٰئِكَ لَا خَلَاقَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ وَلَا يُكَلِّمُهُمُ اللَّهُ** "they shall have no share in the Hereafter, and Allah will not speak to them..." to the end of the verse.

*Khalaq* (الخلاق) is the portion or share (النصيب). *Tazkiyah* (التزكية) — *purification* — is to cause something to grow in a wholesome manner. Since the description used to define this group of people is the opposite of the description used for the other group mentioned in His saying: "whoever fulfills his pledge and is God-conscious," and since the consequences mentioned for their description were negative matters, this implies:

**First:** That the use of the demonstrative pronoun *ula’ika* (اولئك) — *those* — which indicates distance, is to signify the distance of these people from the Presence of Proximity, just as those who fulfill their pledge and are God-conscious are brought near (مقربون) due to Allah's love for them.

**Second:** That the effects of the love of Allah, the Glorified, are the share (الخلاق) in the Hereafter, the speaking (التكليم) and the looking (النظر) on the Day of Resurrection, and the purification (التزكية) and forgiveness, which is the removal of the painful punishment.

The traits that Allah mentioned for these breakers of Allah's covenant and their oaths are three:

One: That they have no share (نصيب) in the Hereafter. What is meant by the Hereafter (الآخرة) is the Abode of the Hereafter (الدار الآخرة) — *the description standing in place of the thing described* — meaning the life after death, just as the world (الدنياً) refers to the Abode of the World (الدار الدنيا), which is the worldly life before death.

The negation of a share for them in the Hereafter is due to their choosing the share of the world over it. From here it becomes clear that what is meant by the "small price" (الثمن القليل) is the world. We previously interpreted it as the "enjoyment of the world" because of His describing it as "small," and He described the enjoyment of the world as such in the saying of the Mightiest of Speakers: **قُلْ مَتَاعُ الدُّنْيَا قَلِيلٌ** "Say, ‘The enjoyment of this world is little’" (4:77); moreover, the enjoyment of the world *is* the world.

Two: That Allah will not speak to them nor look at them on the Day of Resurrection. This has been set in parallel to the divine love for the God-conscious, insofar as love necessitates the lover taking delight in the beloved by freely looking and speaking during presence and union. Since Allah does not love them, He will not speak to them nor look at them on the Day of Resurrection, which is the day of summoning and presence. The progression from speaking to looking is due to the presence of strength and weakness between them, for freedom in speaking is more than in looking; it is as if it were said: "We shall not honor them, neither much nor little."

Three: That Allah will not purify them and they shall have a painful punishment. The absolute nature of the speech implies that what is meant by both is that which encompasses purification and punishment in both this world and the Hereafter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّ مِنْهُمْ لَفَرِيقًا يَلْوُونَ أَلْسِنَتَهُمْ بِالْكِتَابِ لِتَحْسَبُوهُ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ وَمَا هُوَ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ** "And there is a group of them who twist their tongues while [reading] the Book, so that you may think it is from the Book, but it is not from the Book." *Layy* (الليّ) is the twisting of a rope; the twisting of the head or tongue is to incline them. The Exalted said: **لَوَّوْا رُءُوسَهُمْ** "they twist their heads" (63:5), and He said: **لَيًّا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ** "twisting with their tongues" (4:46). It appears that what is meant by this is that they read the discourse they have fabricated against Allah, the Glorified, with the same melodies and intonations with which they read the Book, to deceive people into thinking it is from the Book, while it is not from the Book.

The repetition of the word "the Book" (الكتاب) three times in the speech is to prevent confusion. What is meant by the first "Book" is that which they wrote with their own hands and attributed to Allah, the Glorified. By the second is the Book that Allah sent down through revelation. By the third is the second one; its mention was repeated to prevent confusion and to indicate that the Book, insofar as it is the Book of Allah, is of a status too high to contain such fabrications. This is due to the meaning of the description in the word "the Book" which suggests causality.

Similar to this is the repetition of the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) in His saying: **وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ وَمَا هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ** "and they say, ‘It is from Allah,’ but it is not from Allah." The meaning is: "and it is not from Allah, who is truly God and says nothing but the truth." The Exalted said: **وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ** "and I speak the truth" (38:84).

As for His saying: **وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** "and they attribute lies to Allah, and they know [it]," it is a refutation after a refutation of their attributing their fabricated revelation to Allah, the Glorified. They used to obscure the matter for people through the "twisting of speech" (لحن القول), so Allah nullified it by His saying: "but it is not from the Book." Then they used to say with their tongues: "It is from Allah," so Allah belied them: first, by His saying: "but it is not from Allah," and second, by His saying: "and they attribute lies to Allah." He added to the benefit, first, that lying is their habit and custom, and second, that this is not a lie issued from them due to a confusion in the matter, but rather they are knowledgeable of it and intentional in it.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ تَعَالَوْا إِلَىٰ كَلِمَةٍ سَوَاءٍ** "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word...’" [3:64], it is narrated—meaning by Ibn Jarir—from al-Suddi, who said: "Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم called them—meaning the delegation of the Christians of Najran—and said: 'O People of the Book! Come to a common word...'"
