**I say:** This meaning is also narrated therein from Ibn Jarir from Muhammad ibn Ja'far ibn al-Zubayr. The apparent sense of the narration is that the verse was revealed concerning them. We have already presented the narration at the beginning of the Surah indicating that the beginning of the Surah, up to eighty-odd verses, was revealed concerning the Christians of Najran, and this verse is among them as it occurs before the completion of that number.

It has been mentioned in some narrations that the Messenger of Allah called the Jews of Medina to the "common word" until they accepted the *jizyah* (الجزية) — *the protection tax paid by non-Muslims under Islamic rule* — and that does not contradict the verse being revealed concerning the delegation of Najran.

In *Sahih al-Bukhari*, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Abbas from Abu Sufyan in a long tradition in which he mentions the letter of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم to Heraclius, the Great of Rome. Abu Sufyan said: "Then he—meaning Heraclius—called for the letter of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and read it, and in it was: 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. From Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah, to Heraclius, the Great of Rome. Peace be upon him who follows the guidance. To proceed: I invite you with the invitation of Islam. Submit and you will be safe; submit and Allah will give you your reward twice over. But if you turn away, then upon you is the sin of the peasants (الأريسيين). And O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we worship none but Allah...'—to his saying—'...bear witness that we are Muslims.'" (The tradition).

**I say:** It was also narrated by Muslim in his *Sahih*, and by al-Suyuti in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Nasa'i, Abd al-Razzaq, and Ibn Abi Hatim from Ibn Abbas.

It has been said that the letter of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم to the Muqawqis, the Great of the Copts, also included His saying, the Exalted: "O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you." There is a manuscript attributed to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم written in Kufic script that resembles his letter صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم to Heraclius, and copies of it have recently been made through photography and are in the possession of many.

In any case, historians have mentioned that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم only wrote the letters and sent the messengers to the kings—Caesar, Khosrow, and the Negus—in the sixth year of the Hijrah. This necessitates that the verse was revealed in the sixth year or before it. Historians such as al-Tabari, Ibn al-Athir, and al-Maqrizi have mentioned that the Christians of Najran only came as a delegation to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم in the tenth year of the Hijrah. Others, such as Abu al-Fida in *al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah* and similarly in *al-Sirah al-Halabiyyah*, mentioned that it was in the ninth year of the Hijrah. This would necessitate the revelation of this verse in the ninth or tenth year.

It has sometimes been said that the verse was among those revealed at the beginning of the Hijrah, as suggested by the following narrations. It has also been said that the verse was revealed twice, as reported by Hafiz Ibn Hajar.

What supports the continuity of the Surah's verses in context—as previously indicated at the beginning of the Surah—is that the verse was revealed before the ninth year, and that the story of the delegation occurred in the sixth year of the Hijrah or before it. It is unlikely that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم would write to the greats of Rome, the Copts, and Persia while overlooking Najran despite its proximity.

In the narration, there is another subtle point, which is the beginning of the letter with "In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful." From this, the issue with some of the narrations we quoted regarding the story of the Najran delegation becomes apparent, such as what is reported from al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il*: "That the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم wrote to the people of Najran before *Ta Sin* of Solomon [Surah al-Naml] was revealed to him: 'In the Name of Allah, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. From Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah, to the Bishop of Najran. If you submit, then I praise to you Allah, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. To proceed: I invite you to the worship of Allah instead of the worship of servants, and to the guardianship of Allah instead of the guardianship of servants. If you refuse, then the *jizyah*. If you refuse, then I declare war upon you. Peace.'" (The tradition).

This is because Surah al-Naml is among the Meccan Surahs, and the contents of its verses are explicit in that they were revealed before the Hijrah of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. How can that be reconciled with the story of Najran? Furthermore, the letter contains other matters that cannot be justified, such as the mention of *jizyah* and the declaration of war, and so on. Allah knows best.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Tabarani narrated from Ibn Abbas: "That the letter of the Messenger of Allah to the disbelievers was: 'Come to a common word between us and you...'"

Also in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تُحَاجُّونَ** "O People of the Book! Why do you argue..." [3:65], Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Christians of Najran and the rabbis of the Jews gathered before the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and disputed in his presence. The rabbis said: 'Abraham was nothing but a Jew,' and the Christians said: 'Abraham was nothing but a Christian.' So Allah revealed concerning them: 'O People of the Book! Why do you argue about Abraham, while the Torah and the Evangel were not sent down until after him...'—to His saying—'...and Allah is the Guardian of the faithful.' Then Abu Rafi' al-Qurazi (from the Jews of Banu Qurayzah) said: 'Do you want us, O Muhammad, to worship you as the Christians worship Jesus son of Mary?' A man from the people of Najran said: 'Is that what you want, O Muhammad?' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: 'I seek refuge in Allah from worshipping other than Allah, or commanding the worship of other than Him. I was not sent for that, nor did He command me with it.' So Allah revealed concerning their words: 'It is not for a human that Allah should give him the Book, judgment and prophethood, and then he should say to the people, "Be my servants instead of Allah"’—to His saying—'...after you have been Muslims.' Then He mentioned what He took from them and their forefathers of the covenant to believe in him when he comes to them, and their acknowledgment of it against themselves, saying: 'And when Allah took the covenant of the prophets...'—to His saying—'...of those who bear witness.'"

**I say:** The verses—meaning His saying: "It is not for a human that Allah should give him the Book, judgment and prophethood..." to the end of the verses—are more consistent in context and more easily applicable to Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him) than to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, as will be discussed in the commentary on those verses. Perhaps what is in the narration regarding the revelation of the verses concerning the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم is an inference and application by Ibn Abbas. Moreover, it is the customary practice of the Quran to address this type of statement in the form of question and answer or narration and rebuttal.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Khazin, al-Kalbi narrated from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas, and Muhammad ibn Ishaq narrated it from Ibn Shihab with his chain, the tradition of the migration to Abyssinia. He said: "When Ja'far ibn Abi Talib and a group of the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم migrated to the land of Abyssinia and settled there, and the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم migrated to Medina, and the event of Badr took place, the Quraysh gathered in the Dar al-Nadwa. They said: 'We have a blood-revenge against those with the Negus from the companions of Muhammad for those of you killed at Badr. So collect wealth and gift it to the Negus; perhaps he will hand over to you those of your people who are with him. Let two men of sound judgment be sent to him.'"

"So they sent Amr ibn al-As and Umarah ibn Abi Mu'ayt with gifts: leather and other things. They traveled by sea until they reached Abyssinia. When they entered upon the Negus, they prostrated to him and greeted him. They said to him: 'Our people are sincere advisers to you, grateful, and lovers of your companions. They have sent us to you to warn you against these who have come to you, for they are the people of a lying man who has emerged claiming to be a messenger of Allah, and none of us followed him except the foolish. We had restricted them and forced them into a mountain pass in our land where no one could enter upon them, so hunger and thirst killed them. When the matter became severe for him, he sent his cousin to you to corrupt your religion, your kingdom, and your subjects. So beware of them and hand them over to us so we may deal with them for you.' They said: 'The sign of that is that when they enter upon you, they do not prostrate to you, nor do they greet you with the greeting people greet you with, out of aversion to your religion and tradition.'"

"He said: So the Negus summoned them. When they arrived, Ja'far shouted at the door: 'The party of Allah (حزب الله) seeks permission to enter upon you.' The Negus said: 'Tell this shouter to repeat his words.' Ja'far did so. The Negus said: 'Yes, let them enter under the protection and covenant of Allah.' Amr looked at his companion and said: 'Do you not hear how they jargon about the "party of Allah" and how the King answered them?' That distressed them."

"Then they entered upon him but did not prostrate to him. Amr ibn al-As said: 'Do you not see that they are too arrogant to prostrate to you?' The Negus said to them: 'What prevented you from prostrating to me and greeting me with the greeting with which those who come to me from the horizons greet me?' They said: 'We prostrate to Allah who created you and gave you kingdom. That greeting was for us when we worshipped idols, but Allah sent among us a truthful prophet, and he commanded us with the greeting that Allah is pleased with, which is *al-Salam* (السلام) — *peace* — the greeting of the people of Paradise.' The Negus recognized that this was the truth and that it was in the Torah and the Evangel. He said: 'Which of you was the caller: "The party of Allah seeks permission to enter upon you"?' Ja'far said: 'I am.' He said: 'You are a king among the kings of the earth from the People of the Book; much talk and injustice are not appropriate in your presence. I only wish to answer on behalf of my companions. Command these two men that one of them speaks and the other remains silent so you may hear our dialogue.' Amr said to Ja'far: 'Speak.'"

"Ja'far said to the Negus: 'Ask these two men: are we slaves or free men? If we are slaves who have fled from our masters, then return us to them.' The Negus said: 'Are they slaves or free men?' He said: 'Rather, noble free men.' The Negus said: 'They have escaped slavery.' Ja'far said: 'Ask them: have we shed any blood without right such that retaliation should be taken from us?' Amr said: 'No, not a drop.' Ja'far said: 'Ask them: have we taken people's wealth without right such that we must repay it?' The Negus said: 'If it were a *qintar*, I would be responsible for its repayment.' Amr said: 'No, not even a *qirat* (قيراط) — *a tiny fraction of a coin*.' The Negus said: 'Then what do you seek from them?' He said: 'We and they were upon one religion, the religion of our forefathers, but they left it and followed another. Our people sent us so you may hand them over to us.' The Negus said: 'What is this that you were upon and the religion they followed?' Ja'far said: 'As for the religion we were upon, it was the religion of Satan; we used to disbelieve in Allah and worship stones. As for that which we turned to, it is the religion of Allah, Islam. A messenger came to us with it from Allah, with a Book like the Book of the son of Mary, in agreement with it.' The Negus said: 'O Ja'far, you have spoken of a great matter.'"

"Then the Negus ordered the bell to be rung, and it was rung, and every priest and monk gathered to him. When they gathered, the Negus said: 'I adjure you by Allah who sent down the Evangel to Jesus, do you find between Jesus and the Day of Resurrection a sent prophet?' They said: 'O Allah, yes, he gave us glad tidings.' He said: 'Whoever believes in him has believed in me, and whoever disbelieves in him has disbelieved in me.' The Negus said to Ja'far: 'What does this man say to you? What does he command you and what does he forbid you?' He said: 'He reads to us the Book of Allah, commands us with the right (المعروف) and forbids us from the wrong (المنكر), and commands us with good neighborliness, joining of kinship, and kindness to the orphan. He commands us to worship Allah alone without any partner.' He said to him: 'Read to me from what he reads to you.' So he read to him Surah al-Ankabut and al-Rum, and the eyes of the Negus and his companions overflowed with tears. They said: 'Give us more of this pleasant discourse.' So he read to them Surah al-Kahf. Amr wanted to anger the Negus, so he said: 'They insult Jesus and his mother.' The Negus said: 'What do you say about Jesus and his mother?' So he read to them Surah Maryam. When he reached the mention of Mary and Jesus, the Negus picked up a piece of his toothstick (سواك) the size of a speck in the eye and said: 'By Allah, the Messiah did not exceed what you say by this much.' Then he turned to Ja'far and his companions and said: 'Go, for you are *suyum* (سيوم) in my land'—meaning safe from being insulted or harmed—'and whoever harms you shall be fined.' Then he said: 'Rejoice and do not fear, for there is no *dahurah* (دهورة) — *humiliation or destruction* — today upon the party of Abraham.' Amr said: 'O Negus, and who is the party of Abraham?' He said: 'This group and their companion from whom they came, and whoever follows them.' The polytheists denied that and claimed the religion of Abraham. Then the Negus returned to Amr and his companion the wealth they had brought and said: 'Your gift is but a bribe, so take it back, for Allah gave me kingdom and did not take a bribe from me.' Ja'far said: 'So we departed and were in the best of neighborliness, and Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed concerning that to His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم regarding their dispute over Abraham while he was in Medina: "Indeed the nearest of people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this Prophet and those who believe, and Allah is the Guardian of the faithful."'"

**I say:** This story is narrated through other paths and through the paths of the People of the House (عليه السلام). We have quoted it at length only because it contains important benefits regarding the trials of the Muslims among the first migrants, and it is not strictly an "occasion of revelation" (سبب النزول).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **مَا كَانَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ يَهُودِيًّا وَلَا نَصْرَانِيًّا** "Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian" [3:67], he said: "The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: 'Neither a Jew praying toward the West, nor a Christian praying toward the East, but he was a *hanif* (حنيفاً) — *one naturally inclined toward monotheism* — a Muslim upon the religion of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم.'"

**I say:** The meaning of his being upon the religion of Muhammad (peace be upon them both and their progeny) has been previously explained. The narration considers the facing of the Kaaba, as the *qiblah* (القبلة) — *the direction of prayer* — was turned toward it in Medina, and the Kaaba is approximately at its southern point. The refusal of the Jews and Christians to accept it caused them to deviate from it toward the West, where Jerusalem is, and the East, which the Christians face. Thus, that was considered a deviation from the exact center for both groups. This notion is supported by the wording of the verse: "And thus We have made you a middle nation..." [2:143]. In short, it is a subtle point and nothing more.

In *al-Kafi*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Pure and sincere, containing nothing of the worship of idols."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْرَاهِيمَ** "Indeed the nearest of people to Abraham..." [3:68], the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: "Indeed the nearest of people to the prophets are those who act most in accordance with what they brought." Then he recited this verse and said: "Indeed the friend (ولي) of Muhammad is he who obeys Allah, even if his kinship is distant; and indeed the enemy of Muhammad is he who disobeys Allah, even if his kinship is near."

In *al-Kafi* and the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "They are the Imams and those who follow them."

In the *Tafsirs* of al-Qummi and al-Ayyashi, from Umar ibn Udhaynah from him (peace be upon him), who said: "By Allah, you are from the family of Muhammad." I said: "From their very selves, may I be your ransom?" He said: "Yes, by Allah, from their very selves," three times. Then he looked at me and I looked at him, and he said: "O Umar, indeed Allah says in His Book: 'Indeed the nearest of people...'" (The verse).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالَتْ طَائِفَةٌ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ آمِنُوا** "A group of the People of the Book say, ‘Believe...’" [3:72], from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم came to Medina and was praying toward Jerusalem, that pleased the people. When Allah turned him from Jerusalem to the Sacred House, the Jews were upset by that. The turning of the *qiblah* occurred during the Dhuhr prayer. So they said: 'Muhammad prayed in the morning facing our *qiblah*, so believe in what was revealed to Muhammad at the beginning of the day and disbelieve at its end'—meaning the *qiblah* when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم faced the Sacred Mosque."

**I say:** The narration, as you see, makes the phrase "the beginning of the day" (وجه النهار) an adverb for "revealed" (انزل), rather than for "believe" (آمنوا). This was previously discussed in the preceding commentary.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated through the path of al-Awfi from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: "A group said..." (The verse). He said: "A group of the Jews said: 'When you meet the companions of Muhammad at the beginning of the day, believe; and when it is the end of it, pray your prayer; perhaps they will say: "These are the People of the Book and they are more knowledgeable than us," so perhaps they will turn away from their religion.'"

**I say:** It was also narrated therein from al-Suddi and Mujahid.

In *al-Kafi* (regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed those who sell the covenant of Allah..."), from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: "It was revealed concerning the pledge: 'Indeed those who sell the covenant of Allah and their oaths for a small price, they shall have no share in the Hereafter, and Allah will not speak to them nor look at them on the Day of Resurrection, and they shall have a painful punishment.' *Khalaq* (الخلاق) is the share (النصيب). So whoever has no share in the Hereafter, by what thing shall he enter Paradise?"

In the *Amali* of the Shaykh, with his chain from Adi ibn Adi from his father, who said: "Imru' al-Qays and a man from Hadramawt disputed before the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم regarding a land. He said: 'Do you have evidence?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'Then [it is decided] by his oath.' He said: 'Then, by Allah, he will take my land.' He said: 'If he takes your land by his oath, he will be among those whom Allah will not look at on the Day of Resurrection, nor will He purify him, and he shall have a painful punishment.' He said: The man was terrified and returned it to him."

**I say:** The narration, as you see, does not indicate that the verse was revealed specifically for the occasion of the story. It has been narrated through Sunni paths in several narrations that the verse was revealed regarding this matter, but they are conflicting regarding the details of the story. In some, the dispute was between Imru' al-Qays and a man from Hadramawt, as in the previous narration. In some, it was between al-Ash'ath ibn Qays and a Jewish man regarding a land of his. In some, it was revealed regarding a man from the disbelievers who had displayed his merchandise in the market and swore by Allah that he had been offered for it what he had not been offered, in order to entrap a man from the Muslims; so the verse was revealed.

You have already seen in the preceding commentary that the apparent sense of the verse is that it serves as a causal explanation (تعليل) for the content of the verse preceding it. Thus, the proper approach is to treat the narrations—if possible—as an explanation of the verse's applicability to the occasion of the story, rather than as an "occasion of revelation" in the customary sense.
